map.subtitle: Big Idea 5: Impact of Computing   •   2 days   •   LO IOC-1.A, IOC-1.B
map.intro: Everything for Topic 5.1 lives in this folder. Here is how the three surfaces fit together.
folderItems: Slides — Teacher Edition, Days 1–2 (speaker notes on every slide: timing, cold-call prompts, misconception alerts)
Teacher Guide (pacing with QOTD warm-ups, misconceptions, differentiation, CED alignment, Innovation Investigation #3 framing, auto-derived quiz key)
Guided Notes — Days 1–2 (student fill-in + answer KEY)
Exercise 1: Innovation Effects Dossier — The ShortCut File and Exercise 2: Impact Court (student + answer KEY)
Discussion — Who Answers for the Ripples? (3-part: Opening Day 1, Mid-lesson Day 2, Closing Day 2)
Quiz (student + answer KEY — 8 questions across LO IOC-1.A and IOC-1.B, canonical in the Day-1 master JSON)
map.surfacesHeading: How the three surfaces fit together
surfaces: Slides = the daily instruction you project and teach from (Day 1: how effects work — people, tasks, one effect with two verdicts; Day 2: impact beyond intent and the responsibility line, closing with Common AP Traps and the exit check).
This folder = your private scaffolding — keys, pacing, and facilitation that are deliberately NOT posted to the site.

Website = student practice that paper cannot do: instant grading, per-answer feedback, and mastery tracking. The student versions of the exercises and quiz live online; the answer keys stay with you and the site never displays them.

Scheduling: Topic 5.1 hosts Innovation Investigation #3. Day 2's Stop and Think launches the Create-adjacent write-up (innovation, intended purpose, data, effects) and the flex block gives it work time; the quiz runs as the opener of the NEXT class (spaced retrieval), so the topic never silently consumes a third day.

exercise1.title: Innovation Effects Dossier — The ShortCut File
exercise1.intro: You are the effects analyst for a consumer-technology review board. The (fictional) navigation app ShortCut is up for its annual review, and five documented effects have landed on your desk. For each you will tag the effect beneficial, harmful, or BOTH; rule on whether it was anticipated; and name who is affected. Part A works the dossier; Part B extends the same skills. Everything maps to CB Learning Objectives IOC-1.A and IOC-1.B.
exercise1.scenarioTitle: Effects Dossier: Five Documented Effects Under Review
exercise1.narrative: The board's clerk compiled five effects of ShortCut from surveys, petitions, and agency reports. Your job is to rule on each one before the review hearing. Read the dossier row by row, then answer Part A. Use the three-step method: name the single effect, list everyone it touches, then judge it from each seat before you tag it.

rows: Commutes get shorter
Drivers' survey
Average commute down 11 minutes a day for regular users
Exactly the outcome promised in ShortCut's launch announcement
Cut-through traffic floods Maple Street
Neighborhood association petition
The app routes rush-hour traffic through quiet residential streets
The SAME rerouting that drivers praise is the effect residents protest
Drivers' own navigation skills fade
A five-year user's letter
The author loves the app AND fears being helpless without it
One person holds both verdicts about one effect at once
Ambulances reach patients faster
County EMS report
EMS pipes ShortCut's live congestion data into dispatch routing
ShortCut's team never marketed to — or planned for — emergency services
Insurer raises rates for some drivers
Consumer complaint
Resold location histories were mined for risky-driving patterns
A use of the app's data far outside navigation

exercise1.scenarioNote: Effects adapted from the Day 1 effect-triage table and the Day 2 responsibility section; all terms and EKs follow the College Board AP Computer Science Principles Course and Exam Description (Effective Fall 2023).
exercise1.predict: Before you answer Part A, write one sentence: which one effect in the dossier could appear both in a ShortCut ad and in a lawsuit against ShortCut — and why?
exercise1.partALabel: Part A - Rulings on the Dossier

partA.q: Effects 1 and 2 are the SAME routing behavior seen from two seats. Explain how one effect can be tagged beneficial by drivers and harmful by Maple Street residents — and why 'ShortCut has one good effect and one bad effect' would be the wrong write-up.
partA.answer: The routing engine produces a single effect: traffic moves off congested main roads onto the fastest available streets. Per EK IOC-1.A.4, a single effect can be viewed as both beneficial and harmful by different people — drivers experience it as 11 saved minutes; Maple Street residents experience the identical rerouting as noise, danger, and congestion outside their homes. Writing it up as two separate effects hides the exam's key structure: there is ONE effect, and the verdict depends on which seat you occupy. Full credit requires identifying the shared effect explicitly and giving both viewpoints — not just listing a pro and a con.

partA.q: Effect 3: the letter's author both loves ShortCut and fears being helpless without it. Name the EK idea about tasks changing to incorporate an innovation, and explain how this row shows a single effect judged both ways by the SAME person.
partA.answer: Per EK IOC-1.A.2, the way people complete tasks often changes to incorporate new computing innovations — the author no longer navigates from memory or paper maps; the task 'get somewhere' now runs entirely through the app. That one change IS the effect. Per EK IOC-1.A.4, a single effect can be viewed as both beneficial and harmful even by the same person: the author judges it beneficial (effortless, stress-free travel) and harmful (their own skill has atrophied and the dependence feels risky) simultaneously. Full credit: cite the task-change mechanism AND locate both verdicts inside one person — not split across two people.

partA.q: Effect 4: county EMS pipes ShortCut's live congestion data into ambulance dispatch — a use ShortCut never planned. Rule: was this effect anticipated, and which CED idea about unintended effects does it illustrate? Name the field that advanced.
partA.answer: Unanticipated: not every effect of a computing innovation is anticipated in advance (EK IOC-1.A.3) — nothing in ShortCut's design brief mentioned dispatch, and the dossier notes the team never marketed to emergency services. It illustrates the CED's brighter half of surprise: computing innovations have often had unintended BENEFICIAL effects by leading to advances in other fields (EK IOC-1.B.5). The advanced field is emergency medicine / public safety, where the same live congestion data shortens response times. Full credit requires all three moves — 'unanticipated,' 'unintended beneficial effect,' and the named field; 'lucky accident' with no EK reasoning earns partial credit only.

partA.q: Effect 5: an insurer buys resold location histories, mines them for risky-driving patterns, and raises specific customers' rates. Explain how this parallels the CED's warnings about targeted advertising and data mining, and name the KIND of impact — society, the economy, or culture — this use has.
partA.answer: This is a use far beyond ShortCut's navigation intent (EK IOC-1.B.1), and it mirrors both of the CED's named warnings. Like targeted advertising, data collected to help a business is misused at the individual level (one specific driver's rate goes up) AND the aggregate level (patterns computed across all drivers decide who pays more). Like data mining, information discovered in the data is used to disadvantage people — the same mechanism the CED flags when mined information is used to discriminate against groups of individuals. The impact class is ECONOMIC (EK IOC-1.B.2): money moves against customers who never agreed to become underwriting data. Full credit: the beyond-intent ruling, at least one named parallel with its level(s), and the impact class.

exercise1.partBIntro: Answer in complete sentences using the CED vocabulary. These are the same items available on the site exercise.

partB.q: Of the five effects, which ONE could ShortCut's programmers most reasonably have considered before launch, and which one was practically impossible to foresee? Use the CED's standard for responsible programmers — and its limit.
partB.answer: Strongest pairing: the Maple Street rerouting (Effect 2) was reasonably foreseeable — sending drivers down the fastest available streets obviously includes residential ones, and responsible programmers try to consider the unintended ways their innovations can be used and the effects of those uses (EK IOC-1.B.3); a routing team could have modeled neighborhood impact. The insurer resale chain (Effect 5) — or EMS adoption (Effect 4) — sits at the impossible end: per EK IOC-1.B.4 it is not possible for a programmer to consider ALL the ways an innovation can be used, and third parties repurposing resold data years later is exactly the kind of use no launch checklist contains. Any defensible pairing earns credit if it applies B.3's effort standard to one effect and B.4's impossibility to the other.

partB.q: ShortCut had 10,000 users its first year and Maple Street never noticed. At 20 million users, the petition arrived. Explain what scale changed, citing the CED's point about running a program with a large number of users.
partB.answer: At 10,000 users the rerouting effect existed but was statistically invisible — a few extra cars an hour. Per EK IOC-1.B.6, rapid sharing of a program or running a program with a large number of users can result in significant impacts beyond the intended purpose or control of the programmer: at 20 million users, the same per-user behavior aggregates into a continuous stream of cut-through traffic that a neighborhood physically feels. Scale did not create a new effect; it amplified an existing one past the threshold where a community notices and organizes. The strongest answers also note that the programmers' CONTROL shrank as the user base grew — the effect now exists whether or not ShortCut's team wants it.

partB.q: ENRICHMENT: Using the Collingridge dilemma from the Day 1 deep dive, explain why the moment ShortCut could most easily have changed its routing rules was also the moment it had the least evidence that any change was needed.
partB.answer: Early on (10,000 users), altering the routing algorithm was cheap — few users, no entrenched habits, no business partners — but the neighborhood harm had not yet materialized, so no data pointed at a problem: that is the knowledge side of the dilemma. By the time the petition proved the harm, millions of commuters, advertisers, and even county EMS depended on current behavior, making change slow, costly, and political: the control side. That double bind is the Collingridge dilemma, and it explains why EK IOC-1.A.3's 'not anticipated in advance' is structural rather than an excuse — the evidence that would let creators anticipate an effect is generated by the very adoption that makes the effect hard to undo.

exercise2.title: Impact Court
exercise2.subtitle: 4 cases - LO IOC-1.A, IOC-1.B
exercise2.intro: You are the judge of Impact Court. Four cases arrive, each an innovation accused of causing harm — or suspiciously praised for surprise benefits. For each case, run the sign check (which CED principle is in play and how you know), rule on the mechanism and the creators' defense (what responsible programmers could reasonably have done), and — for advanced credit — name the sub-type of unintended impact. Vague 'technology is good/bad' verdicts score zero.
exercise2.standard: CB Standard (required): sign check + mechanism-and-defense ruling using CED vocabulary, for all 4 cases. Enrichment (optional): also name the sub-type of unintended impact (dual-view effect, cross-field spillover, scale amplification, or aggregate data misuse).
exercise2.methodHeading: Three-Step Method - use for every case
method: Anchor your gut verdict before analyzing. Writing your own ruling first keeps a convincing wrong framing from swaying you.
Step 2 - Find the effect and the seats
Isolate the SINGLE effect at issue and list everyone it touches. The same effect can be beneficial and harmful to different people — or the same person. Judge from each seat before ruling.
Step 3 - Rule on responsibility within the CED's limits
Responsible programmers try to consider unintended uses and their effects — but no programmer can consider them all. Rule on the effort made, never demand omniscience.
exercise2.enrichmentNote: Enrichment (sub-types of unintended impact): DUAL-VIEW EFFECT — one effect, opposite verdicts from different seats; CROSS-FIELD SPILLOVER — the innovation advances a field it was never built for; SCALE AMPLIFICATION — rapid sharing or a huge user base turns a small behavior into a societal impact; AGGREGATE DATA MISUSE — data collected for one purpose is mined or resold to profile and disadvantage people. Real cases often stack two.
fields: Sign check (which CED principle is in play, and what in the case tells you):
Mechanism + defense (how the impact happened, and what responsible creators could reasonably have done):
Enrichment sub-type (which flavor of unintended impact — and does a second one stack?):

scenarios.text: A district rolls out an attendance app that texts parents the moment a student misses any class. Parents call it the district's best purchase in years. School counselors report a spike in students afraid to seek morning counseling help — the instant text reaches volatile homes before any adult can add context. The district asks the court: is the alert feature good or bad?

key.signs: The question itself is the trap — 'good or bad' assumes one verdict exists. There is ONE effect: instant, automatic notification of every absence. Parents view it as beneficial (immediate awareness); vulnerable students experience the identical instant text as harmful (it arrives stripped of context, sometimes into unsafe homes). One effect, two seats — EK IOC-1.A.4.
key.mechanism: The mechanism is automation removing the human buffer: an office call once let a counselor add judgment before a parent heard anything; the app transmits at machine speed to every household, safe or not. Per EK IOC-1.B.3, responsible creators could reasonably have considered this — schools know homes differ — and offered per-student delays or counselor-review routing. The ruling: keep the feature, add the guardrail. Both 'ban the app' and 'the app is fine' fail the dual-view analysis.
key.subtype: DUAL-VIEW EFFECT in textbook form — a single effect judged oppositely from different seats. A faint second layer of SCALE AMPLIFICATION: any one text is minor, but district-wide automation guarantees the worst-case household is reached every single day.

scenarios.text: Two students build a free flashcard app for their AP classes. A celebrity study-influencer features it, and it jumps from 3,000 to 15 million users in ten days. Servers buckle, the shared-deck feature fills with pirated textbook scans, and copyright notices arrive addressed to two teenagers. Their defense: 'We built it for our school.'

key.signs: Rapid sharing at work: per EK IOC-1.B.6, rapid sharing of a program or running it with a large number of users produces significant impacts beyond the intended purpose or CONTROL of the programmer. The tell is the timeline — nothing about the app's code changed in those ten days; only its scale did.
key.mechanism: Every capability was harmless at 3,000 users: shared decks meant classmates trading study notes. At 15 million, the same feature becomes a distribution channel for infringing content faster than two people can moderate. Ruling on the defense: the intent was legitimate and the explosion unforeseeable in timing (EK IOC-1.B.4), but responsibility begins NOW (EK IOC-1.B.3) — add reporting tools, takedown workflows, and upload limits, or gate the growth. 'We built it for our school' explains the past; it does not license ignoring the present.
key.subtype: SCALE AMPLIFICATION is primary — the impact grew out of the user count, not out of any code change. It stacks with a DUAL-VIEW EFFECT: frictionless deck-sharing is simultaneously the beloved feature and the abuse channel — the SAME effect, two verdicts.

scenarios.text: A programmer builds an ad-matching service to help neighborhood shops find nearby customers. A payday-loan chain becomes its biggest client, aiming ads at users whose recent searches include 'overdue rent' and 'car repossessed' — and, across the city, poorer zip codes see loan ads where richer ones see credit-union offers.

key.signs: The CED's targeted-advertising warning verbatim in action: targeted advertising is used to help businesses, but it can be misused at BOTH the individual level (one desperate user singled out by search history) and the aggregate level (whole zip codes systematically steered toward worse financial products) — EK IOC-1.B.1. The neighborhood-by-income pattern also signals a harmful impact on the economy (EK IOC-1.B.2).
key.mechanism: The matching engine does exactly what it was built to do — connect advertisers with receptive audiences; the harm enters through WHO wields it and WHOM it profiles. Ruling: the builder could not foresee every future client (EK IOC-1.B.4), but once the pattern is visible, EK IOC-1.B.3 expects action — extra review for sensitive ad categories, exclusion of predatory verticals, and audits of which demographics receive which offers. The defense 'the algorithm is neutral' fails: neutrality of code does not neutralize a use.
key.subtype: AGGREGATE DATA MISUSE is primary — individuals and whole groups profiled from data gathered to 'help businesses.' It stacks with SCALE AMPLIFICATION: one loan ad is a sales pitch; a city-wide targeting pattern is an economic sorting machine.

scenarios.text: A startup trains a résumé-screening model on ten years of its clients' hiring records to 'find candidates like your best employees.' Independent audits later show it downgrades applicants from two zip codes and one women's college. Meanwhile the same core engine, licensed to a medical lab, is flagging early-stage tumors that radiologists miss. The CEO asks the court: 'So is our technology beneficial or harmful?'

key.signs: Both halves of the CED's machine-learning warning at once: machine learning and data mining have enabled innovation in medicine, business, and science — the tumor-flagging lab — but information discovered in this way has also been used to discriminate against groups of individuals — the two zip codes and the college (EK IOC-1.B.1). Group-level discrimination is harm to SOCIETY, not merely to one applicant (EK IOC-1.B.2).
key.mechanism: One mechanism drives both outcomes: the engine amplifies whatever patterns its training data contains. Ten years of skewed hiring becomes an automated gatekeeper repeating the skew at scale; the same pattern-finding applied to scans becomes earlier diagnoses. Ruling on the CEO's question: it is malformed — effects are judged, not technologies, and this innovation has beneficial AND harmful effects running simultaneously. On responsibility: bias inherited from hiring data is a well-documented, foreseeable risk — EK IOC-1.B.3 expects the audit BEFORE deployment; the medical spillover was a legitimately unforeseeable benefit (EK IOC-1.B.5).
key.subtype: CROSS-FIELD SPILLOVER (a business tool advancing medicine, EK IOC-1.B.5) stacked with AGGREGATE DATA MISUSE (mined patterns disadvantaging groups). The exam-ready observation: the SAME property — pattern amplification — produces the benefit and the harm.

discussion.title: Who Answers for the Ripples?
discussion.minutes: ~25 min total - 3 parts - Opening (Day 1) + Mid-lesson (Day 2) + Closing (Day 2)
discussion.overview: An app saves your commute and ruins a street; a feed entertains you and keeps you up; a model finds tumors and rejects résumés. Across three short windows in this topic, your group will argue about who gets to call an effect 'good,' what an inventor owes the world for uses they never imagined, and whether 'nobody could have known' should ever end the conversation.

parts.name: Part 1: Opening Discussion
parts.when: Day 1, ~8 min, after the bell ringer
parts.intro: In your group of 3-4, discuss both questions (about 3 minutes each), then share your most interesting answer with the class. No written answers required - just discuss and listen.

items.text: Question 1. Look at your starred items — the effects that landed on BOTH your helps and harms lists. Pick the strongest one and argue: is the app 'good for you'? Is that even the right question? What question should replace it?
Question 2. Name a technology effect that you and an adult in your life judge in opposite ways. What does each of you know — or have at stake — that the other doesn't?
Our group's most interesting finding from Part 1 (write 1-2 sentences to share):

parts.name: Part 2: Mid-lesson Discussion
parts.when: Day 2, ~8 min, after the three-named-examples table
parts.intro: In your group, complete both tasks below.

items.label: Task A - The inventor's bill
items.text: The Web's creator expected physics papers and got everything else. Debate: should inventors owe anything for harmful uses they never intended? Agree on ONE sentence stating what a creator owes — and what they can never be billed for.

items.label: Task B - Rank the surprises
items.text: Of the CED's three stories — the Web, targeted advertising, machine learning and data mining — rank them by how foreseeable the misuse was. Record your #1 'should have seen it coming' and the single clue its creators already had.

parts.name: Part 3: Closing Debrief
parts.when: Day 2, ~9 min, after Common AP Traps
parts.intro: As a group, work the prompt below, then one member shares your verdict with the class.

items.label: The 'should have known' tribunal
items.text: A two-person team's app was misused in a way that harmed thousands. Prosecutors say 'you should have known'; the defense says no programmer can consider every use. Build your group's standard: list what EVERY responsible team must do before and after launch — and name the point where responsibility genuinely ends. Test your standard against the flashcard app and the résumé screener from Exercise 2: does it hold for both?
Our group's verdict (the one duty teams most often skip + one-sentence justification):
